text: The surgeon creates a ductotomy on the anterior lateral wall of the cystic duct, extending it as needed to the anterior wall of the common bile duct to facilitate suturing. Bipolar cautery is used to control bleeding during the procedure. Stones are evacuated from the cystic duct by squeezing, followed by irrigation once bile reflux is observed. A cholangiogram is performed using a catheter, and a soft balloon is inserted and inflated to dilate the cystic duct and break valves, ensuring cannulation and stone removal.

The surgeon performs balloon dilation of the cystic duct, ensuring part of the balloon remains outside to break the valves. A colodocuscope is introduced, with pressurized water dripping to maintain visualization. The surgeon encounters difficulty due to undilated valves and uses a wire to guide the balloon for dilation, leaving it inflated for three minutes to ensure proper dilation. An injury to the posterior cystic duct wall is identified and managed with a clip and endo loop. The surgeon extends the ductotomy anteriorly to remove a larger stone from the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses a wire and basket to retrieve stones from the bile duct. The basket is deployed behind the stone, then closed slowly to capture it. The scope is advanced to the duodenum for examination, ensuring no remaining stones. Water flow is adjusted for optimal visualization, and the scope may be flexed to inspect the hepatic ducts depending on the cystic duct insertion.

The surgeon carefully opens a basket behind an impacted stone in the cystic duct, slowly closing it to capture the stone. Due to the stone's impaction, laser lithotripsy is performed to fragment the stone into smaller pieces for removal. The procedure involves minimal invasive techniques, with the surgeon using a scope to visualize and manipulate the stone within the common bile duct. The surgeon examines the duct proximally, observing the right and left hepatic ducts.
